  "content": "{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"Module containing non-deprecated functions borrowed from Numeric.\\n2: \\n3: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n4: import functools\\n5: import types\\n6: import warnings\\n7: \\n8: import numpy as np\\n9: from .._utils import set_module\\n10: from . import multiarray as mu\\n11: from . import overrides\\n12: from . import umath as um\\n13: from . import numerictypes as nt\\n14: from .multiarray import asarray, array, asanyarray, concatenate\\n15: from ._multiarray_umath import _array_converter\\n16: from . import _methods\\n17: \\n18: _dt_ = nt.sctype2char\\n19: \\n20: # functions that are methods\\n21: __all__ = [\\n22:     'all', 'amax', 'amin', 'any', 'argmax',\\n23:     'argmin', 'argpartition', 'argsort', 'around', 'choose', 'clip',\\n24:     'compress', 'cumprod', 'cumsum', 'cumulative_prod', 'cumulative_sum',\\n25:     'diagonal', 'mean', 'max', 'min', 'matrix_transpose',\\n26:     'ndim', 'nonzero', 'partition', 'prod', 'ptp', 'put',\\n27:     'ravel', 'repeat', 'reshape', 'resize', 'round',\\n28:     'searchsorted', 'shape', 'size', 'sort', 'squeeze',\\n29:     'std', 'sum', 'swapaxes', 'take', 'trace', 'transpose', 'var',\\n30: ]\\n31: \\n32: _gentype = types.GeneratorType\\n33: # save away Python sum\\n34: _sum_ = sum\\n35: \\n36: array_function_dispatch = functools.partial(\\n37:     overrides.array_function_dispatch, module='numpy')\\n38: \\n39: \\n40: # functions that are now methods\\n41: def _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds):\\n42:     conv = _array_converter(obj)\\n43:     # As this already tried the method, subok is maybe quite reasonable here\\n44:     # but this follows what was done before. TODO: revisit this.\\n45:     arr, = conv.as_arrays(subok=False)\\n46:     result = getattr(arr, method)(*args, **kwds)\\n47: \\n48:     return conv.wrap(result, to_scalar=False)\\n49: \\n50: \\n51: def _wrapfunc(obj, method, *args, **kwds):\\n52:     bound = getattr(obj, method, None)\\n53:     if bound is None:\\n54:         return _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds)\\n55: \\n56:     try:\\n57:         return bound(*args, **kwds)\\n58:     except TypeError:\\n59:         # A TypeError occurs if the object does have such a method in its\\n60:         # class, but its signature is not identical to that of NumPy's. This\\n61:         # situation has occurred in the case of a downstream library like\\n62:         # 'pandas'.\\n63:         #\\n64:         # Call _wrapit from within the except clause to ensure a potential\\n65:         # exception has a traceback chain.\\n66:         return _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds)\\n67: \\n68: \\n69: def _wrapreduction(obj, ufunc, method, axis, dtype, out, **kwargs):\\n70:     passkwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items()\\n71:                   if v is not np._NoValue}\\n72: \\n73:     if type(obj) is not mu.ndarray:\\n74:         try:\\n75:             reduction = getattr(obj, method)\\n76:         except AttributeError:\\n77:             pass\\n78:         else:\\n79:             # This branch is needed for reductions like any which don't\\n80:             # support a dtype.\\n81:             if dtype is not None:\\n82:                 return reduction(axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, **passkwargs)\\n83:             else:\\n84:                 return reduction(axis=axis, out=out, **passkwargs)\\n85: \\n86:     return ufunc.reduce(obj, axis, dtype, out, **passkwargs)\\n87: \\n88: \\n89: def _wrapreduction_any_all(obj, ufunc, method, axis, out, **kwargs):\\n90:     # Same as above function, but dtype is always bool (but never passed on)\\n91:     passkwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items()\\n92:                   if v is not np._NoValue}\\n93: \\n94:     if type(obj) is not mu.ndarray:\\n95:         try:\\n96:             reduction = getattr(obj, method)\\n97:         except AttributeError:\\n98:             pass\\n99:         else:\\n100:             return reduction(axis=axis, out=out, **passkwargs)\\n101: \\n102:     return ufunc.reduce(obj, axis, bool, out, **passkwargs)\\n103: \\n104: \\n105: def _take_dispatcher(a, indices, axis=None, out=None, mode=None):\\n106:     return (a, out)\\n107: \\n108: \\n109: @array_function_dispatch(_take_dispatcher)\\n110: def take(a, indices, axis=None, out=None, mode='raise'):\\n111:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n112:     Take elements from an array along an axis.\\n113: \\n114:     When axis is not None, this function does the same thing as \\\"fancy\\\"\\n115:     indexing (indexing arrays using arrays); however, it can be easier to use\\n116:     if you need elements along a given axis. A call such as\\n117:     ``np.take(arr, indices, axis=3)`` is equivalent to\\n118:     ``arr[:,:,:,indices,...]``.\\n119: \\n120:     Explained without fancy indexing, this is equivalent to the following use\\n121:     of `ndindex`, which sets each of ``ii``, ``jj``, and ``kk`` to a tuple of\\n122:     indices::\\n123: \\n124:         Ni, Nk = a.shape[:axis], a.shape[axis+1:]\\n125:         Nj = indices.shape\\n126:         for ii in ndindex(Ni):\\n127:             for jj in ndindex(Nj):\\n128:                 for kk in ndindex(Nk):\\n129:                     out[ii + jj + kk] = a[ii + (indices[jj],) + kk]\\n130: \\n131:     Parameters\\n132:     ----------\\n133:     a : array_like (Ni..., M, Nk...)\\n134:         The source array.\\n135:     indices : array_like (Nj...)\\n136:         The indices of the values to extract.\\n137:         Also allow scalars for indices.\\n138:     axis : int, optional\\n139:         The axis over which to select values. By default, the flattened\\n140:         input array is used.\\n141:     out : ndarray, optional (Ni..., Nj..., Nk...)\\n142:         If provided, the result will be placed in this array. It should\\n143:         be of the appropriate shape and dtype. Note that `out` is always\\n144:         buffered if `mode='raise'`; use other modes for better performance.\\n145:     mode : {'raise', 'wrap', 'clip'}, optional\\n146:         Specifies how out-of-bounds indices will behave.\\n147: \\n148:         * 'raise' -- raise an error (default)\\n149:         * 'wrap' -- wrap around\\n150:         * 'clip' -- clip to the range\\n151: \\n152:         'clip' mode means that all indices that are too large are replaced\\n153:         by the index that addresses the last element along that axis. Note\\n154:         that this disables indexing with negative numbers.\\n155: \\n156:     Returns\\n157:     -------\\n158:     out : ndarray (Ni..., Nj..., Nk...)\\n159:         The returned array has the same type as `a`.\\n160: \\n161:     See Also\\n162:     --------\\n163:     compress : Take elements using a boolean mask\\n164:     ndarray.take : equivalent method\\n165:     take_along_axis : Take elements by matching the array and the index arrays\\n166: \\n167:     Notes\\n168:     -----\\n169:     By eliminating the inner loop in the description above, and using `s_` to\\n170:     build simple slice objects, `take` can be expressed  in terms of applying\\n171:     fancy indexing to each 1-d slice::\\n172: \\n173:         Ni, Nk = a.shape[:axis], a.shape[axis+1:]\\n174:         for ii in ndindex(Ni):\\n175:             for kk in ndindex(Nj):\\n176:                 out[ii + s_[...,] + kk] = a[ii + s_[:,] + kk][indices]\\n177: \\n178:     For this reason, it is equivalent to (but faster than) the following use\\n179:     of `apply_along_axis`::\\n180: \\n181:         out = np.apply_along_axis(lambda a_1d: a_1d[indices], axis, a)\\n182: \\n183:     Examples\\n184:     --------\\n185:     >>> import numpy as np\\n186:     >>> a = [4, 3, 5, 7, 6, 8]\\n187:     >>> indices = [0, 1, 4]\\n188:     >>> np.take(a, indices)\\n189:     array([4, 3, 6])\\n190: \\n191:     In this example if `a` is an ndarray, \\\"fancy\\\" indexing can be used.\\n192: \\n193:     >>> a = np.array(a)\\n194:     >>> a[indices]\\n195:     array([4, 3, 6])\\n196: \\n197:     If `indices` is not one dimensional, the output also has these dimensions.\\n198: \\n199:     >>> np.take(a, [[0, 1], [2, 3]])\\n200:     array([[4, 3],\\n201:            [5, 7]])\\n202:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n203:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'take', indices, axis=axis, out=out, mode=mode)\\n204: \\n205: \\n206: def _reshape_dispatcher(a, /, shape=None, order=None, *, newshape=None,\\n207:                         copy=None):\\n208:     return (a,)\\n209: \\n210: \\n211: @array_function_dispatch(_reshape_dispatcher)\\n212: def reshape(a, /, shape=None, order='C', *, newshape=None, copy=None):\\n213:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n214:     Gives a new shape to an array without changing its data.\\n215: \\n216:     Parameters\\n217:     ----------\\n218:     a : array_like\\n219:         Array to be reshaped.\\n220:     shape : int or tuple of ints\\n221:         The new shape should be compatible with the original shape. If\\n222:         an integer, then the result will be a 1-D array of that length.\\n223:         One shape dimension can be -1. In this case, the value is\\n224:         inferred from the length of the array and remaining dimensions.\\n225:     order : {'C', 'F', 'A'}, optional\\n226:         Read the elements of ``a`` using this index order, and place the\\n227:         elements into the reshaped array using this index order. 'C'\\n228:         means to read / write the elements using C-like index order,\\n229:         with the last axis index changing fastest, back to the first\\n230:         axis index changing slowest. 'F' means to read / write the\\n231:         elements using Fortran-like index order, with the first index\\n232:         changing fastest, and the last index changing slowest. Note that\\n233:         the 'C' and 'F' options take no account of the memory layout of\\n234:         the underlying array, and only refer to the order of indexing.\\n235:         'A' means to read / write the elements in Fortran-like index\\n236:         order if ``a`` is Fortran *contiguous* in memory, C-like order\\n237:         otherwise.\\n238:     newshape : int or tuple of ints\\n239:         .. deprecated:: 2.1\\n240:             Replaced by ``shape`` argument. Retained for backward\\n241:             compatibility.\\n242:     copy : bool, optional\\n243:         If ``True``, then the array data is copied. If ``None``, a copy will\\n244:         only be made if it's required by ``order``. For ``False`` it raises\\n245:         a ``ValueError`` if a copy cannot be avoided. Default: ``None``.\\n246: \\n247:     Returns\\n248:     -------\\n249:     reshaped_array : ndarray\\n250:         This will be a new view object if possible; otherwise, it will\\n251:         be a copy.  Note there is no guarantee of the *memory layout* (C- or\\n252:         Fortran- contiguous) of the returned array.\\n253: \\n254:     See Also\\n255:     --------\\n256:     ndarray.reshape : Equivalent method.\\n257: \\n258:     Notes\\n259:     -----\\n260:     It is not always possible to change the shape of an array without copying\\n261:     the data.\\n262: \\n263:     The ``order`` keyword gives the index ordering both for *fetching*\\n264:     the values from ``a``, and then *placing* the values into the output\\n265:     array. For example, let's say you have an array:\\n266: \\n267:     >>> a = np.arange(6).reshape((3, 2))\\n268:     >>> a\\n269:     array([[0, 1],\\n270:            [2, 3],\\n271:            [4, 5]])\\n272: \\n273:     You can think of reshaping as first raveling the array (using the given\\n274:     index order), then inserting the elements from the raveled array into the\\n275:     new array using the same kind of index ordering as was used for the\\n276:     raveling.\\n277: \\n278:     >>> np.reshape(a, (2, 3)) # C-like index ordering\\n279:     array([[0, 1, 2],\\n280:            [3, 4, 5]])\\n281:     >>> np.reshape(np.ravel(a), (2, 3)) # equivalent to C ravel then C reshape\\n282:     array([[0, 1, 2],\\n283:            [3, 4, 5]])\\n284:     >>> np.reshape(a, (2, 3), order='F') # Fortran-like index ordering\\n285:     array([[0, 4, 3],\\n286:            [2, 1, 5]])\\n287:     >>> np.reshape(np.ravel(a, order='F'), (2, 3), order='F')\\n288:     array([[0, 4, 3],\\n289:            [2, 1, 5]])\\n290: \\n291:     Examples\\n292:     --------\\n293:     >>> import numpy as np\\n294:     >>> a = np.array([[1,2,3], [4,5,6]])\\n295:     >>> np.reshape(a, 6)\\n296:     array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])\\n297:     >>> np.reshape(a, 6, order='F')\\n298:     array([1, 4, 2, 5, 3, 6])\\n299: \\n300:     >>> np.reshape(a, (3,-1))       # the unspecified value is inferred to be 2\\n301:     array([[1, 2],\\n302:            [3, 4],\\n303:            [5, 6]])\\n304:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n305:     if newshape is None and shape is None:\\n306:         raise TypeError(\\n307:             \\\"reshape() missing 1 required positional argument: 'shape'\\\")\\n308:     if newshape is not None:\\n309:         if shape is not None:\\n310:             raise TypeError(\\n311:                 \\\"You cannot specify 'newshape' and 'shape' arguments \\\"\\n312:                 \\\"at the same time.\\\")\\n313:         # Deprecated in NumPy 2.1, 2024-04-18\\n314:         warnings.warn(\\n315:             \\\"`newshape` keyword argument is deprecated, \\\"\\n316:             \\\"use `shape=...` or pass shape positionally instead. \\\"\\n317:             \\\"(deprecated in NumPy 2.1)\\\",\\n318:             DeprecationWarning,\\n319:             stacklevel=2,\\n320:         )\\n321:         shape = newshape\\n322:     if copy is not None:\\n323:         return _wrapfunc(a, 'reshape', shape, order=order, copy=copy)\\n324:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'reshape', shape, order=order)\\n325: \\n326: \\n327: def _choose_dispatcher(a, choices, out=None, mode=None):\\n328:     yield a\\n329:     yield from choices\\n330:     yield out\\n331: \\n332: \\n333: @array_function_dispatch(_choose_dispatcher)\\n334: def choose(a, choices, out=None, mode='raise'):\\n335:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n336:     Construct an array from an index array and a list of arrays to choose from.\\n337: \\n338:     First of all, if confused or uncertain, definitely look at the Examples -\\n339:     in its full generality, this function is less simple than it might\\n340:     seem from the following code description::\\n341: \\n342:         np.choose(a,c) == np.array([c[a[I]][I] for I in np.ndindex(a.shape)])\\n343: \\n344:     But this omits some subtleties.  Here is a fully general summary:\\n345: \\n346:     Given an \\\"index\\\" array (`a`) of integers and a sequence of ``n`` arrays\\n347:     (`choices`), `a` and each choice array are first broadcast, as necessary,\\n348:     to arrays of a common shape; calling these *Ba* and *Bchoices[i], i =\\n349:     0,...,n-1* we have that, necessarily, ``Ba.shape == Bchoices[i].shape``\\n350:     for each ``i``.  Then, a new array with shape ``Ba.shape`` is created as\\n351:     follows:\\n352: \\n353:     * if ``mode='raise'`` (the default), then, first of all, each element of\\n354:       ``a`` (and thus ``Ba``) must be in the range ``[0, n-1]``; now, suppose\\n355:       that ``i`` (in that range) is the value at the ``(j0, j1, ..., jm)``\\n356:       position in ``Ba`` - then the value at the same position in the new array\\n357:       is the value in ``Bchoices[i]`` at that same position;\\n358: \\n359:     * if ``mode='wrap'``, values in `a` (and thus `Ba`) may be any (signed)\\n360:       integer; modular arithmetic is used to map integers outside the range\\n361:       `[0, n-1]` back into that range; and then the new array is constructed\\n362:       as above;\\n363: \\n364:     * if ``mode='clip'``, values in `a` (and thus ``Ba``) may be any (signed)\\n365:       integer; negative integers are mapped to 0; values greater than ``n-1``\\n366:       are mapped to ``n-1``; and then the new array is constructed as above.\\n367: \\n368:     Parameters\\n369:     ----------\\n370:     a : int array\\n371:         This array must contain integers in ``[0, n-1]``, where ``n`` is the\\n372:         number of choices, unless ``mode=wrap`` or ``mode=clip``, in which\\n373:         cases any integers are permissible.\\n374:     choices : sequence of arrays\\n375:         Choice arrays. `a` and all of the choices must be broadcastable to the\\n376:         same shape.  If `choices` is itself an array (not recommended), then\\n377:         its outermost dimension (i.e., the one corresponding to\\n378:         ``choices.shape[0]``) is taken as defining the \\\"sequence\\\".\\n379:     out : array, optional\\n380:         If provided, the result will be inserted into this array. It should\\n381:         be of the appropriate shape and dtype. Note that `out` is always\\n382:         buffered if ``mode='raise'``; use other modes for better performance.\\n383:     mode : {'raise' (default), 'wrap', 'clip'}, optional\\n384:         Specifies how indices outside ``[0, n-1]`` will be treated:\\n385: \\n386:         * 'raise' : an exception is raised\\n387:         * 'wrap' : value becomes value mod ``n``\\n388:         * 'clip' : values < 0 are mapped to 0, values > n-1 are mapped to n-1\\n389: \\n390:     Returns\\n391:     -------\\n392:     merged_array : array\\n393:         The merged result.\\n394: \\n395:     Raises\\n396:     ------\\n397:     ValueError: shape mismatch\\n398:         If `a` and each choice array are not all broadcastable to the same\\n399:         shape.\\n400: \\n401:     See Also\\n402:     --------\\n403:     ndarray.choose : equivalent method\\n404:     numpy.take_along_axis : Preferable if `choices` is an array\\n405: \\n406:     Notes\\n407:     -----\\n408:     To reduce the chance of misinterpretation, even though the following\\n409:     \\\"abuse\\\" is nominally supported, `choices` should neither be, nor be\\n410:     thought of as, a single array, i.e., the outermost sequence-like container\\n411:     should be either a list or a tuple.\\n412: \\n413:     Examples\\n414:     --------\\n415: \\n416:     >>> import numpy as np\\n417:     >>> choices = [[0, 1, 2, 3], [10, 11, 12, 13],\\n418:     ...   [20, 21, 22, 23], [30, 31, 32, 33]]\\n419:     >>> np.choose([2, 3, 1, 0], choices\\n420:     ... # the first element of the result will be the first element of the\\n421:     ... # third (2+1) \\\"array\\\" in choices, namely, 20; the second element\\n422:     ... # will be the second element of the fourth (3+1) choice array, i.e.,\\n423:     ... # 31, etc.\\n424:     ... )\\n425:     array([20, 31, 12,  3])\\n426:     >>> np.choose([2, 4, 1, 0], choices, mode='clip') # 4 goes to 3 (4-1)\\n427:     array([20, 31, 12,  3])\\n428:     >>> # because there are 4 choice arrays\\n429:     >>> np.choose([2, 4, 1, 0], choices, mode='wrap') # 4 goes to (4 mod 4)\\n430:     array([20,  1, 12,  3])\\n431:     >>> # i.e., 0\\n432: \\n433:     A couple examples illustrating how choose broadcasts:\\n434: \\n435:     >>> a = [[1, 0, 1], [0, 1, 0], [1, 0, 1]]\\n436:     >>> choices = [-10, 10]\\n437:     >>> np.choose(a, choices)\\n438:     array([[ 10, -10,  10],\\n439:            [-10,  10, -10],\\n440:            [ 10, -10,  10]])\\n441: \\n442:     >>> # With thanks to Anne Archibald\\n443:     >>> a = np.array([0, 1]).reshape((2,1,1))\\n444:     >>> c1 = np.array([1, 2, 3]).reshape((1,3,1))\\n445:     >>> c2 = np.array([-1, -2, -3, -4, -5]).reshape((1,1,5))\\n446:     >>> np.choose(a, (c1, c2)) # result is 2x3x5, res[0,:,:]=c1, res[1,:,:]=c2\\n447:     array([[[ 1,  1,  1,  1,  1],\\n448:             [ 2,  2,  2,  2,  2],\\n449:             [ 3,  3,  3,  3,  3]],\\n450:            [[-1, -2, -3, -4, -5],\\n451:             [-1, -2, -3, -4, -5],\\n452:             [-1, -2, -3, -4, -5]]])\\n453: \\n454:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n455:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'choose', choices, out=out, mode=mode)\\n456: \\n457: \\n458: def _repeat_dispatcher(a, repeats, axis=None):\\n459:     return (a,)\\n460: \\n461: \\n462: @array_function_dispatch(_repeat_dispatcher)\\n463: def repeat(a, repeats, axis=None):\\n464:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n465:     Repeat each element of an array after themselves\\n466: \\n467:     Parameters\\n468:     ----------\\n469:     a : array_like\\n470:         Input array.\\n471:     repeats : int or array of ints\\n472:         The number of repetitions for each element.  `repeats` is broadcasted\\n473:         to fit the shape of the given axis.\\n474:     axis : int, optional\\n475:         The axis along which to repeat values.  By default, use the\\n476:         flattened input array, and return a flat output array.\\n477: \\n478:     Returns\\n479:     -------\\n480:     repeated_array : ndarray\\n481:         Output array which has the same shape as `a`, except along\\n482:         the given axis.\\n483: \\n484:     See Also\\n485:     --------\\n486:     tile : Tile an array.\\n487:     unique : Find the unique elements of an array.\\n488: \\n489:     Examples\\n490:     --------\\n491:     >>> import numpy as np\\n492:     >>> np.repeat(3, 4)\\n493:     array([3, 3, 3, 3])\\n494:     >>> x = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]])\\n495:     >>> np.repeat(x, 2)\\n496:     array([1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4])\\n497:     >>> np.repeat(x, 3, axis=1)\\n498:     array([[1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2],\\n499:            [3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4]])\\n500:     >>> np.repeat(x, [1, 2], axis=0)\\n501: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py%3FL1-500\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "1: \"\"\"Module containing non-deprecated functions borrowed from Numeric.\n2: \n3: \"\"\"\n4: import functools\n5: import types\n6: import warnings\n7: \n8: import numpy as np\n9: from .._utils import set_module\n10: from . import multiarray as mu\n11: from . import overrides\n12: from . import umath as um\n13: from . import numerictypes as nt\n14: from .multiarray import asarray, array, asanyarray, concatenate\n15: from ._multiarray_umath import _array_converter\n16: from . import _methods\n17: \n18: _dt_ = nt.sctype2char\n19: \n20: # functions that are methods\n21: __all__ = [\n22:     'all', 'amax', 'amin', 'any', 'argmax',\n23:     'argmin', 'argpartition', 'argsort', 'around', 'choose', 'clip',\n24:     'compress', 'cumprod', 'cumsum', 'cumulative_prod', 'cumulative_sum',\n25:     'diagonal', 'mean', 'max', 'min', 'matrix_transpose',\n26:     'ndim', 'nonzero', 'partition', 'prod', 'ptp', 'put',\n27:     'ravel', 'repeat', 'reshape', 'resize', 'round',\n28:     'searchsorted', 'shape', 'size', 'sort', 'squeeze',\n29:     'std', 'sum', 'swapaxes', 'take', 'trace', 'transpose', 'var',\n30: ]\n31: \n32: _gentype = types.GeneratorType\n33: # save away Python sum\n34: _sum_ = sum\n35: \n36: array_function_dispatch = functools.partial(\n37:     overrides.array_function_dispatch, module='numpy')\n38: \n39: \n40: # functions that are now methods\n41: def _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds):\n42:     conv = _array_converter(obj)\n43:     # As this already tried the method, subok is maybe quite reasonable here\n44:     # but this follows what was done before. TODO: revisit this.\n45:     arr, = conv.as_arrays(subok=False)\n46:     result = getattr(arr, method)(*args, **kwds)\n47: \n48:     return conv.wrap(result, to_scalar=False)\n49: \n50: \n51: def _wrapfunc(obj, method, *args, **kwds):\n52:     bound = getattr(obj, method, None)\n53:     if bound is None:\n54:         return _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds)\n55: \n56:     try:\n57:         return bound(*args, **kwds)\n58:     except TypeError:\n59:         # A TypeError occurs if the object does have such a method in its\n60:         # class, but its signature is not identical to that of NumPy's. This\n61:         # situation has occurred in the case of a downstream library like\n62:         # 'pandas'.\n63:         #\n64:         # Call _wrapit from within the except clause to ensure a potential\n65:         # exception has a traceback chain.\n66:         return _wrapit(obj, method, *args, **kwds)\n67: \n68: \n69: def _wrapreduction(obj, ufunc, method, axis, dtype, out, **kwargs):\n70:     passkwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items()\n71:                   if v is not np._NoValue}\n72: \n73:     if type(obj) is not mu.ndarray:\n74:         try:\n75:             reduction = getattr(obj, method)\n76:         except AttributeError:\n77:             pass\n78:         else:\n79:             # This branch is needed for reductions like any which don't\n80:             # support a dtype.\n81:             if dtype is not None:\n82:                 return reduction(axis=axis, dtype=dtype, out=out, **passkwargs)\n83:             else:\n84:                 return reduction(axis=axis, out=out, **passkwargs)\n85: \n86:     return ufunc.reduce(obj, axis, dtype, out, **passkwargs)\n87: \n88: \n89: def _wrapreduction_any_all(obj, ufunc, method, axis, out, **kwargs):\n90:     # Same as above function, but dtype is always bool (but never passed on)\n91:     passkwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items()\n92:                   if v is not np._NoValue}\n93: \n94:     if type(obj) is not mu.ndarray:\n95:         try:\n96:             reduction = getattr(obj, method)\n97:         except AttributeError:\n98:             pass\n99:         else:\n100:             return reduction(axis=axis, out=out, **passkwargs)\n101: \n102:     return ufunc.reduce(obj, axis, bool, out, **passkwargs)\n103: \n104: \n105: def _take_dispatcher(a, indices, axis=None, out=None, mode=None):\n106:     return (a, out)\n107: \n108: \n109: @array_function_dispatch(_take_dispatcher)\n110: def take(a, indices, axis=None, out=None, mode='raise'):\n111:     \"\"\"\n112:     Take elements from an array along an axis.\n113: \n114:     When axis is not None, this function does the same thing as \"fancy\"\n115:     indexing (indexing arrays using arrays); however, it can be easier to use\n116:     if you need elements along a given axis. A call such as\n117:     ``np.take(arr, indices, axis=3)`` is equivalent to\n118:     ``arr[:,:,:,indices,...]``.\n119: \n120:     Explained without fancy indexing, this is equivalent to the following use\n121:     of `ndindex`, which sets each of ``ii``, ``jj``, and ``kk`` to a tuple of\n122:     indices::\n123: \n124:         Ni, Nk = a.shape[:axis], a.shape[axis+1:]\n125:         Nj = indices.shape\n126:         for ii in ndindex(Ni):\n127:             for jj in ndindex(Nj):\n128:                 for kk in ndindex(Nk):\n129:                     out[ii + jj + kk] = a[ii + (indices[jj],) + kk]\n130: \n131:     Parameters\n132:     ----------\n133:     a : array_like (Ni..., M, Nk...)\n134:         The source array.\n135:     indices : array_like (Nj...)\n136:         The indices of the values to extract.\n137:         Also allow scalars for indices.\n138:     axis : int, optional\n139:         The axis over which to select values. By default, the flattened\n140:         input array is used.\n141:     out : ndarray, optional (Ni..., Nj..., Nk...)\n142:         If provided, the result will be placed in this array. It should\n143:         be of the appropriate shape and dtype. Note that `out` is always\n144:         buffered if `mode='raise'`; use other modes for better performance.\n145:     mode : {'raise', 'wrap', 'clip'}, optional\n146:         Specifies how out-of-bounds indices will behave.\n147: \n148:         * 'raise' -- raise an error (default)\n149:         * 'wrap' -- wrap around\n150:         * 'clip' -- clip to the range\n151: \n152:         'clip' mode means that all indices that are too large are replaced\n153:         by the index that addresses the last element along that axis. Note\n154:         that this disables indexing with negative numbers.\n155: \n156:     Returns\n157:     -------\n158:     out : ndarray (Ni..., Nj..., Nk...)\n159:         The returned array has the same type as `a`.\n160: \n161:     See Also\n162:     --------\n163:     compress : Take elements using a boolean mask\n164:     ndarray.take : equivalent method\n165:     take_along_axis : Take elements by matching the array and the index arrays\n166: \n167:     Notes\n168:     -----\n169:     By eliminating the inner loop in the description above, and using `s_` to\n170:     build simple slice objects, `take` can be expressed  in terms of applying\n171:     fancy indexing to each 1-d slice::\n172: \n173:         Ni, Nk = a.shape[:axis], a.shape[axis+1:]\n174:         for ii in ndindex(Ni):\n175:             for kk in ndindex(Nj):\n176:                 out[ii + s_[...,] + kk] = a[ii + s_[:,] + kk][indices]\n177: \n178:     For this reason, it is equivalent to (but faster than) the following use\n179:     of `apply_along_axis`::\n180: \n181:         out = np.apply_along_axis(lambda a_1d: a_1d[indices], axis, a)\n182: \n183:     Examples\n184:     --------\n185:     >>> import numpy as np\n186:     >>> a = [4, 3, 5, 7, 6, 8]\n187:     >>> indices = [0, 1, 4]\n188:     >>> np.take(a, indices)\n189:     array([4, 3, 6])\n190: \n191:     In this example if `a` is an ndarray, \"fancy\" indexing can be used.\n192: \n193:     >>> a = np.array(a)\n194:     >>> a[indices]\n195:     array([4, 3, 6])\n196: \n197:     If `indices` is not one dimensional, the output also has these dimensions.\n198: \n199:     >>> np.take(a, [[0, 1], [2, 3]])\n200:     array([[4, 3],\n201:            [5, 7]])\n202:     \"\"\"\n203:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'take', indices, axis=axis, out=out, mode=mode)\n204: \n205: \n206: def _reshape_dispatcher(a, /, shape=None, order=None, *, newshape=None,\n207:                         copy=None):\n208:     return (a,)\n209: \n210: \n211: @array_function_dispatch(_reshape_dispatcher)\n212: def reshape(a, /, shape=None, order='C', *, newshape=None, copy=None):\n213:     \"\"\"\n214:     Gives a new shape to an array without changing its data.\n215: \n216:     Parameters\n217:     ----------\n218:     a : array_like\n219:         Array to be reshaped.\n220:     shape : int or tuple of ints\n221:         The new shape should be compatible with the original shape. If\n222:         an integer, then the result will be a 1-D array of that length.\n223:         One shape dimension can be -1. In this case, the value is\n224:         inferred from the length of the array and remaining dimensions.\n225:     order : {'C', 'F', 'A'}, optional\n226:         Read the elements of ``a`` using this index order, and place the\n227:         elements into the reshaped array using this index order. 'C'\n228:         means to read / write the elements using C-like index order,\n229:         with the last axis index changing fastest, back to the first\n230:         axis index changing slowest. 'F' means to read / write the\n231:         elements using Fortran-like index order, with the first index\n232:         changing fastest, and the last index changing slowest. Note that\n233:         the 'C' and 'F' options take no account of the memory layout of\n234:         the underlying array, and only refer to the order of indexing.\n235:         'A' means to read / write the elements in Fortran-like index\n236:         order if ``a`` is Fortran *contiguous* in memory, C-like order\n237:         otherwise.\n238:     newshape : int or tuple of ints\n239:         .. deprecated:: 2.1\n240:             Replaced by ``shape`` argument. Retained for backward\n241:             compatibility.\n242:     copy : bool, optional\n243:         If ``True``, then the array data is copied. If ``None``, a copy will\n244:         only be made if it's required by ``order``. For ``False`` it raises\n245:         a ``ValueError`` if a copy cannot be avoided. Default: ``None``.\n246: \n247:     Returns\n248:     -------\n249:     reshaped_array : ndarray\n250:         This will be a new view object if possible; otherwise, it will\n251:         be a copy.  Note there is no guarantee of the *memory layout* (C- or\n252:         Fortran- contiguous) of the returned array.\n253: \n254:     See Also\n255:     --------\n256:     ndarray.reshape : Equivalent method.\n257: \n258:     Notes\n259:     -----\n260:     It is not always possible to change the shape of an array without copying\n261:     the data.\n262: \n263:     The ``order`` keyword gives the index ordering both for *fetching*\n264:     the values from ``a``, and then *placing* the values into the output\n265:     array. For example, let's say you have an array:\n266: \n267:     >>> a = np.arange(6).reshape((3, 2))\n268:     >>> a\n269:     array([[0, 1],\n270:            [2, 3],\n271:            [4, 5]])\n272: \n273:     You can think of reshaping as first raveling the array (using the given\n274:     index order), then inserting the elements from the raveled array into the\n275:     new array using the same kind of index ordering as was used for the\n276:     raveling.\n277: \n278:     >>> np.reshape(a, (2, 3)) # C-like index ordering\n279:     array([[0, 1, 2],\n280:            [3, 4, 5]])\n281:     >>> np.reshape(np.ravel(a), (2, 3)) # equivalent to C ravel then C reshape\n282:     array([[0, 1, 2],\n283:            [3, 4, 5]])\n284:     >>> np.reshape(a, (2, 3), order='F') # Fortran-like index ordering\n285:     array([[0, 4, 3],\n286:            [2, 1, 5]])\n287:     >>> np.reshape(np.ravel(a, order='F'), (2, 3), order='F')\n288:     array([[0, 4, 3],\n289:            [2, 1, 5]])\n290: \n291:     Examples\n292:     --------\n293:     >>> import numpy as np\n294:     >>> a = np.array([[1,2,3], [4,5,6]])\n295:     >>> np.reshape(a, 6)\n296:     array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])\n297:     >>> np.reshape(a, 6, order='F')\n298:     array([1, 4, 2, 5, 3, 6])\n299: \n300:     >>> np.reshape(a, (3,-1))       # the unspecified value is inferred to be 2\n301:     array([[1, 2],\n302:            [3, 4],\n303:            [5, 6]])\n304:     \"\"\"\n305:     if newshape is None and shape is None:\n306:         raise TypeError(\n307:             \"reshape() missing 1 required positional argument: 'shape'\")\n308:     if newshape is not None:\n309:         if shape is not None:\n310:             raise TypeError(\n311:                 \"You cannot specify 'newshape' and 'shape' arguments \"\n312:                 \"at the same time.\")\n313:         # Deprecated in NumPy 2.1, 2024-04-18\n314:         warnings.warn(\n315:             \"`newshape` keyword argument is deprecated, \"\n316:             \"use `shape=...` or pass shape positionally instead. \"\n317:             \"(deprecated in NumPy 2.1)\",\n318:             DeprecationWarning,\n319:             stacklevel=2,\n320:         )\n321:         shape = newshape\n322:     if copy is not None:\n323:         return _wrapfunc(a, 'reshape', shape, order=order, copy=copy)\n324:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'reshape', shape, order=order)\n325: \n326: \n327: def _choose_dispatcher(a, choices, out=None, mode=None):\n328:     yield a\n329:     yield from choices\n330:     yield out\n331: \n332: \n333: @array_function_dispatch(_choose_dispatcher)\n334: def choose(a, choices, out=None, mode='raise'):\n335:     \"\"\"\n336:     Construct an array from an index array and a list of arrays to choose from.\n337: \n338:     First of all, if confused or uncertain, definitely look at the Examples -\n339:     in its full generality, this function is less simple than it might\n340:     seem from the following code description::\n341: \n342:         np.choose(a,c) == np.array([c[a[I]][I] for I in np.ndindex(a.shape)])\n343: \n344:     But this omits some subtleties.  Here is a fully general summary:\n345: \n346:     Given an \"index\" array (`a`) of integers and a sequence of ``n`` arrays\n347:     (`choices`), `a` and each choice array are first broadcast, as necessary,\n348:     to arrays of a common shape; calling these *Ba* and *Bchoices[i], i =\n349:     0,...,n-1* we have that, necessarily, ``Ba.shape == Bchoices[i].shape``\n350:     for each ``i``.  Then, a new array with shape ``Ba.shape`` is created as\n351:     follows:\n352: \n353:     * if ``mode='raise'`` (the default), then, first of all, each element of\n354:       ``a`` (and thus ``Ba``) must be in the range ``[0, n-1]``; now, suppose\n355:       that ``i`` (in that range) is the value at the ``(j0, j1, ..., jm)``\n356:       position in ``Ba`` - then the value at the same position in the new array\n357:       is the value in ``Bchoices[i]`` at that same position;\n358: \n359:     * if ``mode='wrap'``, values in `a` (and thus `Ba`) may be any (signed)\n360:       integer; modular arithmetic is used to map integers outside the range\n361:       `[0, n-1]` back into that range; and then the new array is constructed\n362:       as above;\n363: \n364:     * if ``mode='clip'``, values in `a` (and thus ``Ba``) may be any (signed)\n365:       integer; negative integers are mapped to 0; values greater than ``n-1``\n366:       are mapped to ``n-1``; and then the new array is constructed as above.\n367: \n368:     Parameters\n369:     ----------\n370:     a : int array\n371:         This array must contain integers in ``[0, n-1]``, where ``n`` is the\n372:         number of choices, unless ``mode=wrap`` or ``mode=clip``, in which\n373:         cases any integers are permissible.\n374:     choices : sequence of arrays\n375:         Choice arrays. `a` and all of the choices must be broadcastable to the\n376:         same shape.  If `choices` is itself an array (not recommended), then\n377:         its outermost dimension (i.e., the one corresponding to\n378:         ``choices.shape[0]``) is taken as defining the \"sequence\".\n379:     out : array, optional\n380:         If provided, the result will be inserted into this array. It should\n381:         be of the appropriate shape and dtype. Note that `out` is always\n382:         buffered if ``mode='raise'``; use other modes for better performance.\n383:     mode : {'raise' (default), 'wrap', 'clip'}, optional\n384:         Specifies how indices outside ``[0, n-1]`` will be treated:\n385: \n386:         * 'raise' : an exception is raised\n387:         * 'wrap' : value becomes value mod ``n``\n388:         * 'clip' : values < 0 are mapped to 0, values > n-1 are mapped to n-1\n389: \n390:     Returns\n391:     -------\n392:     merged_array : array\n393:         The merged result.\n394: \n395:     Raises\n396:     ------\n397:     ValueError: shape mismatch\n398:         If `a` and each choice array are not all broadcastable to the same\n399:         shape.\n400: \n401:     See Also\n402:     --------\n403:     ndarray.choose : equivalent method\n404:     numpy.take_along_axis : Preferable if `choices` is an array\n405: \n406:     Notes\n407:     -----\n408:     To reduce the chance of misinterpretation, even though the following\n409:     \"abuse\" is nominally supported, `choices` should neither be, nor be\n410:     thought of as, a single array, i.e., the outermost sequence-like container\n411:     should be either a list or a tuple.\n412: \n413:     Examples\n414:     --------\n415: \n416:     >>> import numpy as np\n417:     >>> choices = [[0, 1, 2, 3], [10, 11, 12, 13],\n418:     ...   [20, 21, 22, 23], [30, 31, 32, 33]]\n419:     >>> np.choose([2, 3, 1, 0], choices\n420:     ... # the first element of the result will be the first element of the\n421:     ... # third (2+1) \"array\" in choices, namely, 20; the second element\n422:     ... # will be the second element of the fourth (3+1) choice array, i.e.,\n423:     ... # 31, etc.\n424:     ... )\n425:     array([20, 31, 12,  3])\n426:     >>> np.choose([2, 4, 1, 0], choices, mode='clip') # 4 goes to 3 (4-1)\n427:     array([20, 31, 12,  3])\n428:     >>> # because there are 4 choice arrays\n429:     >>> np.choose([2, 4, 1, 0], choices, mode='wrap') # 4 goes to (4 mod 4)\n430:     array([20,  1, 12,  3])\n431:     >>> # i.e., 0\n432: \n433:     A couple examples illustrating how choose broadcasts:\n434: \n435:     >>> a = [[1, 0, 1], [0, 1, 0], [1, 0, 1]]\n436:     >>> choices = [-10, 10]\n437:     >>> np.choose(a, choices)\n438:     array([[ 10, -10,  10],\n439:            [-10,  10, -10],\n440:            [ 10, -10,  10]])\n441: \n442:     >>> # With thanks to Anne Archibald\n443:     >>> a = np.array([0, 1]).reshape((2,1,1))\n444:     >>> c1 = np.array([1, 2, 3]).reshape((1,3,1))\n445:     >>> c2 = np.array([-1, -2, -3, -4, -5]).reshape((1,1,5))\n446:     >>> np.choose(a, (c1, c2)) # result is 2x3x5, res[0,:,:]=c1, res[1,:,:]=c2\n447:     array([[[ 1,  1,  1,  1,  1],\n448:             [ 2,  2,  2,  2,  2],\n449:             [ 3,  3,  3,  3,  3]],\n450:            [[-1, -2, -3, -4, -5],\n451:             [-1, -2, -3, -4, -5],\n452:             [-1, -2, -3, -4, -5]]])\n453: \n454:     \"\"\"\n455:     return _wrapfunc(a, 'choose', choices, out=out, mode=mode)\n456: \n457: \n458: def _repeat_dispatcher(a, repeats, axis=None):\n459:     return (a,)\n460: \n461: \n462: @array_function_dispatch(_repeat_dispatcher)\n463: def repeat(a, repeats, axis=None):\n464:     \"\"\"\n465:     Repeat each element of an array after themselves\n466: \n467:     Parameters\n468:     ----------\n469:     a : array_like\n470:         Input array.\n471:     repeats : int or array of ints\n472:         The number of repetitions for each element.  `repeats` is broadcasted\n473:         to fit the shape of the given axis.\n474:     axis : int, optional\n475:         The axis along which to repeat values.  By default, use the\n476:         flattened input array, and return a flat output array.\n477: \n478:     Returns\n479:     -------\n480:     repeated_array : ndarray\n481:         Output array which has the same shape as `a`, except along\n482:         the given axis.\n483: \n484:     See Also\n485:     --------\n486:     tile : Tile an array.\n487:     unique : Find the unique elements of an array.\n488: \n489:     Examples\n490:     --------\n491:     >>> import numpy as np\n492:     >>> np.repeat(3, 4)\n493:     array([3, 3, 3, 3])\n494:     >>> x = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]])\n495:     >>> np.repeat(x, 2)\n496:     array([1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4])\n497:     >>> np.repeat(x, 3, axis=1)\n498:     array([[1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2],\n499:            [3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4]])\n500:     >>> np.repeat(x, [1, 2], axis=0)\n501: ",
    "url": "https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2@9ca88bff35bf60de74f9f95e6f90e6b4e0913e78/-/blob/numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py%3FL1-500"
  }
}

{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/bulk_update.sh << 'EOFSCRIPT'\n#!/bin/bash\n\n# Create the directory structure\nmkdir -p /workspace/numpy/{_core,lib,linalg,fft,ma} /workspace/benchmarks/benchmarks /workspace/doc/{neps,changelog}\n\n# Base URL for raw Sourcegraph files\nBASE_URL=\"https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/numpy--v2.2.2/-/raw\"\n\n# Files to fetch and update\ndeclare -a FILES=(\n    \"numpy/_core/numeric.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/shape_base.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/function_base.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/defchararray.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/einsumfunc.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/arrayprint.py\"\n    \"numpy/_core/_asarray.py\"\n    \"numpy/linalg/_linalg.py\"\n    \"numpy/fft/_pocketfft.py\"\n    \"numpy/fft/_helper.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_npyio_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_polynomial_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_shape_base_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_type_check_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_histograms_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_arraysetops_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_twodim_base_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_arraypad_impl.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/recfunctions.py\"\n    \"numpy/lib/_index_tricks_impl.py\"\n    \"benchmarks/benchmarks/bench_overrides.py\"\n)\n\necho \"Fetching files from Sourcegraph...\"\nfor file in \"${FILES[@]}\"; do\n    url=\"${BASE_URL}/${file}\"\n    echo \"Fetching: $file\"\n    if curl -s \"$url\" -o \"/workspace/$file\"; then\n        echo \"  \u2713 Fetched\"\n    else\n        echo \"  \u2717 Failed to fetch\"\n    fi\ndone\n\necho \"\"\necho \"Updating content with sed...\"\ncd /workspace\n\n# Replace all occurrences\nfind . -name \"*.py\" -type f -exec sed -i \\\n    -e 's/array_function_dispatch = functools\\.partial(\\n    overrides\\.array_function_dispatch,/dispatch_array_function = functools.partial(\\n    overrides.dispatch_array_function,/g' \\\n    -e 's/@array_function_dispatch(/@dispatch_array_function(/g' \\\n    -e 's/ array_function_dispatch(/ dispatch_array_function(/g' \\\n    -e 's/from numpy\\._core\\.overrides import array_function_dispatch/from numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function/g' \\\n    -e 's/overrides\\.array_function_dispatch/overrides.dispatch_array_function/g' \\\n    {} \\;\n\necho \"Done!\"\nEOFSCRIPT\n\nchmod +x /tmp/bulk_update.sh\n#/tmp/bulk_update.sh  # Temporarily disabled to test curl first\n",
  "description": "Create bulk update script using curl and sed"
}

{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/update_files.py << 'EOFPYTHON'\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"Update array_function_dispatch to dispatch_array_function in Python files\"\"\"\n\nimport os\nimport re\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\n# Files to update\nfiles_to_update = [\n    'numpy/_core/numeric.py',\n    'numpy/_core/fromnumeric.py',\n    'numpy/_core/shape_base.py',\n    'numpy/_core/function_base.py',\n    'numpy/_core/defchararray.py',\n    'numpy/_core/einsumfunc.py',\n    'numpy/_core/arrayprint.py',\n    'numpy/_core/_asarray.py',\n    'numpy/linalg/_linalg.py',\n    'numpy/fft/_pocketfft.py',\n    'numpy/fft/_helper.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_npyio_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_polynomial_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_shape_base_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_type_check_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_histograms_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_arraysetops_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_twodim_base_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_scimath_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_arraypad_impl.py',\n    'numpy/lib/recfunctions.py',\n    'numpy/lib/_index_tricks_impl.py',\n    'benchmarks/benchmarks/bench_overrides.py',\n]\n\nreplacements = [\n    # Replace local variable assignment\n    (r'array_function_dispatch = functools\\.partial\\(\\n    overrides\\.array_function_dispatch,',\n     'dispatch_array_function = functools.partial(\\n    overrides.dispatch_array_function,'),\n    # Replace imports\n    (r'from numpy\\._core\\.overrides import array_function_dispatch',\n     'from numpy._core.overrides import dispatch_array_function'),\n    # Replace decorator usage\n    (r'@array_function_dispatch\\(',\n     '@dispatch_array_function('),\n    # Replace function calls\n    (r' array_function_dispatch\\(',\n     ' dispatch_array_function('),\n    # Replace module references\n    (r'overrides\\.array_function_dispatch',\n     'overrides.dispatch_array_function'),\n]\n\ndef update_file(filepath):\n    \"\"\"Update a single file\"\"\"\n    if not os.path.exists(filepath):\n        return False\n    \n    try:\n        with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n            content = f.read()\n        \n        original_content = content\n        \n        # Apply replacements\n        for pattern, replacement in replacements:\n            content = re.sub(pattern, replacement, content)\n        \n        # Only write if content changed\n        if content != original_content:\n            with open(filepath, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n                f.write(content)\n            return True\n        return False\n    except Exception as e:\n        print(f\"Error updating {filepath}: {e}\")\n        return False\n\n# Change to workspace directory\nos.chdir('/workspace')\n\n# Update files\nupdated_count = 0\nskipped_count = 0\n\nfor filepath in files_to_update:\n    if update_file(filepath):\n        print(f\"\u2713 Updated: {filepath}\")\n        updated_count += 1\n    else:\n        print(f\"- Skipped (file not found): {filepath}\")\n        skipped_count += 1\n\nprint(f\"\\nSummary: {updated_count} files updated, {skipped_count} files skipped\")\nEOFPYTHON\n\npython3 /tmp/update_files.py\n",
  "description": "Create and run Python script to update files in workspace"
}

{
  "description": "Fetch and update NumPy dispatch files",

Output
"{\"content\":\"1: __all__ = ['atleast_1d', 'atleast_2d', 'atleast_3d', 'block', 'hstack',\\n2:            'stack', 'unstack', 'vstack']\\n3: \\n4: import functools\\n5: import itertools\\n6: import operator\\n7: \\n8: from . import numeric as _nx\\n9: from . import overrides\\n10: from .multiarray import array, asanyarray, normalize_axis_index\\n11: from . import fromnumeric as _from_nx\\n12: \\n13: array_function_dispatch = functools.partial(\\n14:     overrides.array_function_dispatch, module='numpy')\\n15: \\n16: \\n17: def _atleast_1d_dispatcher(*arys):\\n18:     return arys\\n19: \\n20: \\n21: @array_function_dispatch(_atleast_1d_dispatcher)\\n22: def atleast_1d(*arys):\\n23:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n24:     Convert inputs to arrays with at least one dimension.\\n25: \\n26:     Scalar inputs are converted to 1-dimensional arrays, whilst\\n27:     higher-dimensional inputs are preserved.\\n28: \\n29:     Parameters\\n30:     ----------\\n31:     arys1, arys2, ... : array_like\\n32:         One or more input arrays.\\n33: \\n34:     Returns\\n35:     -------\\n36:     ret : ndarray\\n37:         An array, or tuple of arrays, each with ``a.ndim >= 1``.\\n38:         Copies are made only if necessary.\\n39: \\n40:     See Also\\n41:     --------\\n42:     atleast_2d, atleast_3d\\n43: \\n44:     Examples\\n45:     --------\\n46:     >>> import numpy as np\\n47:     >>> np.atleast_1d(1.0)\\n48:     array([1.])\\n49: \\n50:     >>> x = np.arange(9.0).reshape(3,3)\\n51:     >>> np.atleast_1d(x)\\n52:     array([[0., 1., 2.],\\n53:            [3., 4., 5.],\\n54:            [6., 7., 8.]])\\n55:     >>> np.atleast_1d(x) is x\\n56:     True\\n57: \\n58:     >>> np.atleast_1d(1, [3, 4])\\n59:     (array([1]), array([3, 4]))\\n60: \\n61:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n62:     if len(arys) == 1:\\n63:         result = asanyarray(arys[0])\\n64:         if result.ndim == 0:\\n65:             result = result.reshape(1)\\n66:         return result\\n67:     res = []\\n68:     for ary in arys:\\n69:         result = asanyarray(ary)\\n70:         if result.ndim == 0:\\n71:             result = result.reshape(1)\\n72:         res.append(result)\\n73:     return tuple(res)\\n74: \\n75: \\n76: def _atleast_2d_dispatcher(*arys):\\n77:     return arys\\n78: \\n79: \\n80: @array_function_dispatch(_atleast_2d_dispatcher)\\n81: def atleast_2d(*arys):\\n82:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n83:     View inputs as arrays with at least two dimensions.\\n84: \\n85:     Parameters\\n86:     ----------\\n87:     arys1, arys2, ... : array_like\\n88:         One or more array-like sequences.  Non-array inputs are converted\\n89:         to arrays.  Arrays that already have two or more dimensions are\\n90:         preserved.\\n91: \\n92:     Returns\\n93:     -------\\n94:     res, res2, ... : ndarray\\n95:         An array, or tuple of arrays, each with ``a.ndim >= 2``.\\n96:         Copies are avoided where possible, and views with two or more\\n97:         dimensions are returned.\\n98: \\n99:     See Also\\n100:     --------\\n101:     atleast_1d, atleast_3d\\n102: \\n103:     Examples\\n104:     --------\\n105:     >>> import numpy as np\\n106:     >>> np.atleast_2d(3.0)\\n107:     array([[3.]])\\n108: \\n109:     >>> x = np.arange(3.0)\\n110:     >>> np.atleast_2d(x)\\n111:     array([[0., 1., 2.]])\\n112:     >>> np.atleast_2d(x).base is x\\n113:     True\\n114: \\n115:     >>> np.atleast_2d(1, [1, 2], [[1, 2]])\\n116:     (array([[1]]), array([[1, 2]]), array([[1, 2]]))\\n117: \\n118:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n119:     res = []\\n120:     for ary in arys:\\n121:         ary = asanyarray(ary)\\n122:         if ary.ndim == 0:\\n123:             result = ary.reshape(1, 1)\\n124:         elif ary.ndim == 1:\\n125:             result = ary[_nx.newaxis, :]\\n126:         else:\\n127:             result = ary\\n128:         res.append(result)\\n129:     if len(res) == 1:\\n130:         return res[0]\\n131:     else:\\n132:         return tuple(res)\\n133: \\n134: \\n135: def _atleast_3d_dispatcher(*arys):\\n136:     return arys\\n137: \\n138: \\n139: @array_function_dispatch(_atleast_3d_dispatcher)\\n140: def atleast_3d(*arys):\\n141:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n142:     View inputs as arrays with at least three dimensions.\\n143: \\n144:     Parameters\\n145:     ----------\\n146:     arys1, arys2, ... : array_like\\n147:         One or more array-like sequences.  Non-array inputs are converted to\\n148:         arrays.  Arrays that already have three or more dimensions are\\n149:         preserved.\\n150: \\n151:     Returns\\n152:     -------\\n153:     res1, res2, ... : ndarray\\n154:         An array, or tuple of arrays, each with ``a.ndim >= 3``.  Copies are\\n155:         avoided where possible, and views with three or more dimensions are\\n156:         returned.  For example, a 1-D array of shape ``(N,)`` becomes a view\\n157:         of shape ``(1, N, 1)``, and a 2-D array of shape ``(M, N)`` becomes a\\n158:         view of shape ``(M, N, 1)``.\\n159: \\n160:     See Also\\n161:     --------\\n162:     atleast_1d, atleast_2d\\n163: \\n164:     Examples\\n165:     --------\\n166:     >>> import numpy as np\\n167:     >>> np.atleast_3d(3.0)\\n168:     array([[[3.]]])\\n169: \\n170:     >>> x = np.arange(3.0)\\n171:     >>> np.atleast_3d(x).shape\\n172:     (1, 3, 1)\\n173: \\n174:     >>> x = np.arange(12.0).reshape(4,3)\\n175:     >>> np.atleast_3d(x).shape\\n176:     (4, 3, 1)\\n177:     >>> np.atleast_3d(x).base is x.base  # x is a reshape, so not base itself\\n178:     True\\n179: \\n180:     >>> for arr in np.atleast_3d([1, 2], [[1, 2]], [[[1, 2]]]):\\n181:     ...     print(arr, arr.shape) # doctest: +SKIP\\n182:     ...\\n183:     [[[1]\\n184:       [2]]] (1, 2, 1)\\n185:     [[[1]\\n186:       [2]]] (1, 2, 1)\\n187:     [[[1 2]]] (1, 1, 2)\\n188: \\n189:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n190:     res = []\\n191:     for ary in arys:\\n192:         ary = asanyarray(ary)\\n193:         if ary.ndim == 0:\\n194:             result = ary.reshape(1, 1, 1)\\n195:         elif ary.ndim == 1:\\n196:             result = ary[_nx.newaxis, :, _nx.newaxis]\\n197:         elif ary.ndim == 2:\\n198:             result = ary[:, :, _nx.newaxis]\\n199:         else:\\n200:             result = ary\\n201:         res.append(result)\\n202:     if len(res) == 1:\\n203:         return res[0]\\n204:     else:\\n205:         return tuple(res)\\n206: \\n207: \\n208: def _arrays_for_stack_dispatcher(arrays):\\n209:     if not hasattr(arrays, \\\"__getitem__\\\"):\\n210:         raise TypeError('arrays to stack must be passed as a \\\"sequence\\\" type '\\n211:                         'such as list or tuple.')\\n212: \\n213:     return tuple(arrays)\\n214: \\n215: \\n216: def _vhstack_dispatcher(tup, *, dtype=None, casting=None):\\n217:     return _arrays_for_stack_dispatcher(tup)\\n218: \\n219: \\n220: @array_function_dispatch(_vhstack_dispatcher)\\n221: def vstack(tup, *, dtype=None, casting=\\\"same_kind\\\"):\\n222:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n223:     Stack arrays in sequence vertically (row wise).\\n224: \\n225:     This is equivalent to concatenation along the first axis after 1-D arrays\\n226:     of shape `(N,)` have been reshaped to `(1,N)`. Rebuilds arrays divided by\\n227:     `vsplit`.\\n228: \\n229:     This function makes most sense for arrays with up to 3 dimensions. For\\n230:     instance, for pixel-data with a height (first axis), width (second axis),\\n231:     and r/g/b channels (third axis). The functions `concatenate`, `stack` and\\n232:     `block` provide more general stacking and concatenation operations.\\n233: \\n234:     Parameters\\n235:     ----------\\n236:     tup : sequence of ndarrays\\n237:         The arrays must have the same shape along all but the first axis.\\n238:         1-D arrays must have the same length. In the case of a single\\n239:         array_like input, it will be treated as a sequence of arrays; i.e.,\\n240:         each element along the zeroth axis is treated as a separate array.\\n241: \\n242:     dtype : str or dtype\\n243:         If provided, the destination array will have this dtype. Cannot be\\n244:         provided together with `out`.\\n245: \\n246:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n247: \\n248:     casting : {'no', 'equiv', 'safe', 'same_kind', 'unsafe'}, optional\\n249:         Controls what kind of data casting may occur. Defaults to 'same_kind'.\\n250: \\n251:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n252: \\n253:     Returns\\n254:     -------\\n255:     stacked : ndarray\\n256:         The array formed by stacking the given arrays, will be at least 2-D.\\n257: \\n258:     See Also\\n259:     --------\\n260:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n261:     stack : Join a sequence of arrays along a new axis.\\n262:     block : Assemble an nd-array from nested lists of blocks.\\n263:     hstack : Stack arrays in sequence horizontally (column wise).\\n264:     dstack : Stack arrays in sequence depth wise (along third axis).\\n265:     column_stack : Stack 1-D arrays as columns into a 2-D array.\\n266:     vsplit : Split an array into multiple sub-arrays vertically (row-wise).\\n267:     unstack : Split an array into a tuple of sub-arrays along an axis.\\n268: \\n269:     Examples\\n270:     --------\\n271:     >>> import numpy as np\\n272:     >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3])\\n273:     >>> b = np.array([4, 5, 6])\\n274:     >>> np.vstack((a,b))\\n275:     array([[1, 2, 3],\\n276:            [4, 5, 6]])\\n277: \\n278:     >>> a = np.array([[1], [2], [3]])\\n279:     >>> b = np.array([[4], [5], [6]])\\n280:     >>> np.vstack((a,b))\\n281:     array([[1],\\n282:            [2],\\n283:            [3],\\n284:            [4],\\n285:            [5],\\n286:            [6]])\\n287: \\n288:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n289:     arrs = atleast_2d(*tup)\\n290:     if not isinstance(arrs, tuple):\\n291:         arrs = (arrs,)\\n292:     return _nx.concatenate(arrs, 0, dtype=dtype, casting=casting)\\n293: \\n294: \\n295: @array_function_dispatch(_vhstack_dispatcher)\\n296: def hstack(tup, *, dtype=None, casting=\\\"same_kind\\\"):\\n297:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n298:     Stack arrays in sequence horizontally (column wise).\\n299: \\n300:     This is equivalent to concatenation along the second axis, except for 1-D\\n301:     arrays where it concatenates along the first axis. Rebuilds arrays divided\\n302:     by `hsplit`.\\n303: \\n304:     This function makes most sense for arrays with up to 3 dimensions. For\\n305:     instance, for pixel-data with a height (first axis), width (second axis),\\n306:     and r/g/b channels (third axis). The functions `concatenate`, `stack` and\\n307:     `block` provide more general stacking and concatenation operations.\\n308: \\n309:     Parameters\\n310:     ----------\\n311:     tup : sequence of ndarrays\\n312:         The arrays must have the same shape along all but the second axis,\\n313:         except 1-D arrays which can be any length. In the case of a single\\n314:         array_like input, it will be treated as a sequence of arrays; i.e.,\\n315:         each element along the zeroth axis is treated as a separate array.\\n316: \\n317:     dtype : str or dtype\\n318:         If provided, the destination array will have this dtype. Cannot be\\n319:         provided together with `out`.\\n320: \\n321:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n322: \\n323:     casting : {'no', 'equiv', 'safe', 'same_kind', 'unsafe'}, optional\\n324:         Controls what kind of data casting may occur. Defaults to 'same_kind'.\\n325: \\n326:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n327: \\n328:     Returns\\n329:     -------\\n330:     stacked : ndarray\\n331:         The array formed by stacking the given arrays.\\n332: \\n333:     See Also\\n334:     --------\\n335:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n336:     stack : Join a sequence of arrays along a new axis.\\n337:     block : Assemble an nd-array from nested lists of blocks.\\n338:     vstack : Stack arrays in sequence vertically (row wise).\\n339:     dstack : Stack arrays in sequence depth wise (along third axis).\\n340:     column_stack : Stack 1-D arrays as columns into a 2-D array.\\n341:     hsplit : Split an array into multiple sub-arrays\\n342:              horizontally (column-wise).\\n343:     unstack : Split an array into a tuple of sub-arrays along an axis.\\n344: \\n345:     Examples\\n346:     --------\\n347:     >>> import numpy as np\\n348:     >>> a = np.array((1,2,3))\\n349:     >>> b = np.array((4,5,6))\\n350:     >>> np.hstack((a,b))\\n351:     array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])\\n352:     >>> a = np.array([[1],[2],[3]])\\n353:     >>> b = np.array([[4],[5],[6]])\\n354:     >>> np.hstack((a,b))\\n355:     array([[1, 4],\\n356:            [2, 5],\\n357:            [3, 6]])\\n358: \\n359:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n360:     arrs = atleast_1d(*tup)\\n361:     if not isinstance(arrs, tuple):\\n362:         arrs = (arrs,)\\n363:     # As a special case, dimension 0 of 1-dimensional arrays is \\\"horizontal\\\"\\n364:     if arrs and arrs[0].ndim == 1:\\n365:         return _nx.concatenate(arrs, 0, dtype=dtype, casting=casting)\\n366:     else:\\n367:         return _nx.concatenate(arrs, 1, dtype=dtype, casting=casting)\\n368: \\n369: \\n370: def _stack_dispatcher(arrays, axis=None, out=None, *,\\n371:                       dtype=None, casting=None):\\n372:     arrays = _arrays_for_stack_dispatcher(arrays)\\n373:     if out is not None:\\n374:         # optimize for the typical case where only arrays is provided\\n375:         arrays = list(arrays)\\n376:         arrays.append(out)\\n377:     return arrays\\n378: \\n379: \\n380: @array_function_dispatch(_stack_dispatcher)\\n381: def stack(arrays, axis=0, out=None, *, dtype=None, casting=\\\"same_kind\\\"):\\n382:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n383:     Join a sequence of arrays along a new axis.\\n384: \\n385:     The ``axis`` parameter specifies the index of the new axis in the\\n386:     dimensions of the result. For example, if ``axis=0`` it will be the first\\n387:     dimension and if ``axis=-1`` it will be the last dimension.\\n388: \\n389:     Parameters\\n390:     ----------\\n391:     arrays : sequence of ndarrays\\n392:         Each array must have the same shape. In the case of a single ndarray\\n393:         array_like input, it will be treated as a sequence of arrays; i.e.,\\n394:         each element along the zeroth axis is treated as a separate array.\\n395: \\n396:     axis : int, optional\\n397:         The axis in the result array along which the input arrays are stacked.\\n398: \\n399:     out : ndarray, optional\\n400:         If provided, the destination to place the result. The shape must be\\n401:         correct, matching that of what stack would have returned if no\\n402:         out argument were specified.\\n403: \\n404:     dtype : str or dtype\\n405:         If provided, the destination array will have this dtype. Cannot be\\n406:         provided together with `out`.\\n407: \\n408:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n409: \\n410:     casting : {'no', 'equiv', 'safe', 'same_kind', 'unsafe'}, optional\\n411:         Controls what kind of data casting may occur. Defaults to 'same_kind'.\\n412: \\n413:         .. versionadded:: 1.24\\n414: \\n415: \\n416:     Returns\\n417:     -------\\n418:     stacked : ndarray\\n419:         The stacked array has one more dimension than the input arrays.\\n420: \\n421:     See Also\\n422:     --------\\n423:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n424:     block : Assemble an nd-array from nested lists of blocks.\\n425:     split : Split array into a list of multiple sub-arrays of equal size.\\n426:     unstack : Split an array into a tuple of sub-arrays along an axis.\\n427: \\n428:     Examples\\n429:     --------\\n430:     >>> import numpy as np\\n431:     >>> rng = np.random.default_rng()\\n432:     >>> arrays = [rng.normal(size=(3,4)) for _ in range(10)]\\n433:     >>> np.stack(arrays, axis=0).shape\\n434:     (10, 3, 4)\\n435: \\n436:     >>> np.stack(arrays, axis=1).shape\\n437:     (3, 10, 4)\\n438: \\n439:     >>> np.stack(arrays, axis=2).shape\\n440:     (3, 4, 10)\\n441: \\n442:     >>> a = np.array([1, 2, 3])\\n443:     >>> b = np.array([4, 5, 6])\\n444:     >>> np.stack((a, b))\\n445:     array([[1, 2, 3],\\n446:            [4, 5, 6]])\\n447: \\n448:     >>> np.stack((a, b), axis=-1)\\n449:     array([[1, 4],\\n450:            [2, 5],\\n451:            [3, 6]])\\n452: \\n453:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n454:     arrays = [asanyarray(arr) for arr in arrays]\\n455:     if not arrays:\\n456:         raise ValueError('need at least one array to stack')\\n457: \\n458:     shapes = {arr.shape for arr in arrays}\\n459:     if len(shapes) != 1:\\n460:         raise ValueError('all input arrays must have the same shape')\\n461: \\n462:     result_ndim = arrays[0].ndim + 1\\n463:     axis = normalize_axis_index(axis, result_ndim)\\n464: \\n465:     sl = (slice(None),) * axis + (_nx.newaxis,)\\n466:     expanded_arrays = [arr[sl] for arr in arrays]\\n467:     return _nx.concatenate(expanded_arrays, axis=axis, out=out,\\n468:                            dtype=dtype, casting=casting)\\n469: \\n470: def _unstack_dispatcher(x, /, *, axis=None):\\n471:     return (x,)\\n472: \\n473: @array_function_dispatch(_unstack_dispatcher)\\n474: def unstack(x, /, *, axis=0):\\n475:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n476:     Split an array into a sequence of arrays along the given axis.\\n477: \\n478:     The ``axis`` parameter specifies the dimension along which the array will\\n479:     be split. For example, if ``axis=0`` (the default) it will be the first\\n480:     dimension and if ``axis=-1`` it will be the last dimension.\\n481: \\n482:     The result is a tuple of arrays split along ``axis``.\\n483: \\n484:     .. versionadded:: 2.1.0\\n485: \\n486:     Parameters\\n487:     ----------\\n488:     x : ndarray\\n489:         The array to be unstacked.\\n490:     axis : int, optional\\n491:         Axis along which the array will be split. Default: ``0``.\\n492: \\n493:     Returns\\n494:     -------\\n495:     unstacked : tuple of ndarrays\\n496:         The unstacked arrays.\\n497: \\n498:     See Also\\n499:     --------\\n500:     stack : Join a sequence of arrays along a new axis.\\n501:     concatenate : Join a sequence of arrays along an existing axis.\\n502:     block : Assemble an nd-array from nested lists of blocks.\\n503:     split : Split array into a list of multiple sub-arrays of equal size.\\n504: \\n505:     Notes\\n506:     -----\\n507:     ``unstack`` serves as the reverse operation of :py:func:`stack`, i.e.,\\n508:     ``stack(unstack(x, axis=axis), axis=axis) == x``.\\n509: \\n510:     This function is equivalent to ``tuple(np.moveaxis(x, axis, 0))``, since\\n511:     iterating on an array iterates along the first axis.\\n512: \\n513:     Examples\\n514:     --------\\n515:     >>> arr = np.arange(24).reshape((2, 3, 4))\\n516:     >>> np.unstack(arr)\\n517:     (array([[ 0,  1,  2,  3],\\n518:             [ 4,  5,  6,  7],\\n519:             [ 8,  9, 10, 11]]),\\n520:      array([[12, 13, 14, 15],\\n521:             [16, 17, 18, 19],\\n522:             [20, 21, 22, 23]]))\\n523:     >>> np.unstack(arr, axis=1)\\n524:     (array([[ 0,  1,  2,  3],\\n525:             [12, 13, 14, 15]]),\\n526:      array([[ 4,  5,  6,  7],\\n527:             [16, 17, 18, 19]]),\\n528:      array([[ 8,  9, 10, 11],\\n529:             [20, 21, 22, 23]]))\\n530:     >>> arr2 = np.stack(np.unstack(arr, axis=1), axis=1)\\n531:     >>> arr2.shape\\n532:     (2, 3, 4)\\n533:     >>> np.all(arr == arr2)\\n534:     np.True_\\n535: \\n536:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n537:     if x.ndim == 0:\\n538:         raise ValueError(\\\"Input array must be at least 1-d.\\\")\\n539:     return tuple(_nx.moveaxis(x, axis, 0))\\n540: \\n541: # Internal functions to eliminate the overhead of repeated dispatch in one of\\n542: # the two possible paths inside np.block.\\n543: # Use getattr to protect against __array_function__ being disabled.\\n544: _size = getattr(_from_nx.size, '__wrapped__', _from_nx.size)\\n545: _ndim = getattr(_from_nx.ndim, '__wrapped__', _from_nx.ndim)\\n546: _concatenate = getattr(_from_nx.concatenate,\\n547:                        '__wrapped__', _from_nx.concatenate)\\n548: \\n549: \\n550: def _block_format_index(index):\\n551:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n552:     Convert a list of indices ``[0, 1, 2]`` into ``\\\"arrays[0][1][2]\\\"``.\\n553:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n554:     idx_str = ''.join('[{}]'.format(i) for i in index if i is not None)\\n555:     return 'arrays' + idx_str\\n556: \\n557: \\n558: def _block_check_depths_match(arrays, parent_index=[]):\\n559:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n560:     Recursive function checking that the depths of nested lists in `arrays`\\n561:     all match. Mismatch raises a ValueError as described in the block\\n562:     docstring below.\\n563: \\n564:     The entire index (rather than just the depth) needs to be calculated\\n565:     for each innermost list, in case an error needs to be raised, so that\\n566:     the index of the offending list can be printed as part of the error.\\n567: \\n568:     Parameters\\n569:     ----------\\n570:     arrays : nested list of arrays\\n571:         The arrays to check\\n572:     parent_index : list of int\\n573:         The full index of `arrays` within the nested lists passed to\\n574:         `_block_check_depths_match` at the top of the recursion.\\n575: \\n576:     Returns\\n577:     -------\\n578:     first_index : list of int\\n579:         The full index of an element from the bottom of the nesting in\\n580:         `arrays`. If any element at the bottom is an empty list, this will\\n581:         refer to it, and the last index along the empty axis will be None.\\n582:     max_arr_ndim : int\\n583:         The maximum of the ndims of the arrays nested in `arrays`.\\n584:     final_size: int\\n585:         The number of elements in the final array. This is used the motivate\\n586:         the choice of algorithm used using benchmarking wisdom.\\n587: \\n588:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n589:     if type(arrays) is tuple:\\n590:         # not strictly necessary, but saves us from:\\n591:         #  - more than one way to do things - no point treating tuples like\\n592:         #    lists\\n593:         #  - horribly confusing behaviour that results when tuples are\\n594:         #    treated like ndarray\\n595:         raise TypeError(\\n596:             '{} is a tuple. '\\n597:             'Only lists can be used to arrange blocks, and np.block does '\\n598:             'not allow implicit conversion from tuple to ndarray.'.format(\\n599:                 _block_format_index(parent_index)\\n600:             )\\n601:         )\\n602:     elif type(arrays) is list and len(arrays) > 0:\\n603:         idxs_ndims = (_block_check_depths_match(arr, parent_index + [i])\\n604:                       for i, arr in enumerate(arrays))\\n605: \\n606:         first_index, max_arr_ndim, final_size = next(idxs_ndims)\\n607:         for index, ndim, size in idxs_ndims:\\n608:             final_size += size\\n609:             if ndim > max_arr_ndim:\\n610:                 max_arr_ndim = ndim\\n611:             if len(index) != len(first_index):\\n612:                 raise ValueError(\\n613:                     \\\"List depths are mismatched. First element was at depth \\\"\\n614:                     \\\"{}, but there is an element at depth {} ({})\\\".format(\\n615:                         len(first_index),\\n616:                         len(index),\\n617:                         _block_format_index(index)\\n618:                     )\\n619:                 )\\n620:             # propagate our flag that indicates an empty list at the bottom\\n621:             if index[-1] is None:\\n622:                 first_index = index\\n623: \\n624:         return first_index, max_arr_ndim, final_size\\n625:     elif type(arrays) is list and len(arrays) == 0:\\n626:         # We've 'bottomed out' on an empty list\\n627:         return parent_index + [None], 0, 0\\n628:     else:\\n629:         # We've 'bottomed out' - arrays is either a scalar or an array\\n630:         size = _size(arrays)\\n631:         return parent_index, _ndim(arrays), size\\n632: \\n633: \\n634: def _atleast_nd(a, ndim):\\n635:     # Ensures `a` has at least `ndim` dimensions by prepending\\n636:     # ones to `a.shape` as necessary\\n637:     return array(a, ndmin=ndim, copy=None, subok=True)\\n638: \\n639: \\n640: def _accumulate(values):\\n641:     return list(itertools.accumulate(values))\\n642: \\n643: \\n644: def _concatenate_shapes(shapes, axis):\\n645:     \\\"\\\"\\\"Given array shapes, return the resulting shape and slices prefixes.\\n646: \\n647:     These help in nested concatenation.\\n648: \\n649:     Returns\\n650:     -------\\n651:     shape: tuple of int\\n652:         This tuple satisfies::\\n653: \\n654:             shape, _ = _concatenate_shapes([arr.shape for shape in arrs], axis)\\n655:             shape == concatenate(arrs, axis).shape\\n656: \\n657:     slice_prefixes: tuple of (slice(start, end), )\\n658:         For a list of arrays being concatenated, this returns the slice\\n659:         in the larger array at axis that needs to be sliced into.\\n660: \\n661:         For example, the following holds::\\n662: \\n663:             ret = concatenate([a, b, c], axis)\\n664:             _, (sl_a, sl_b, sl_c) = concatenate_slices([a, b, c], axis)\\n665: \\n666:             ret[(slice(None),) * axis + sl_a] == a\\n667:             ret[(slice(None),) * axis + sl_b] == b\\n668:             ret[(slice(None),) * axis + sl_c] == c\\n669: \\n670:         These are called slice prefixes since they are used in the recursive\\n671:         blocking algorithm to compute the left-most slices during the\\n672:         recursion. Therefore, they must be prepended to rest of the slice\\n673:         that was computed deeper in the recursion.\\n674: \\n675:         These are returned as tuples to ensure that they can quickly be added\\n676:         to existing slice tuple without creating a new tuple every time.\\n677: \\n678:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n679:     # Cache a result that will be reused.\\n680:     shape_at_axis = [shape[axis] for shape in shapes]\\n681: \\n682:     # Take a shape, any shape\\n683:     first_shape = shapes[0]\\n684:     first_shape_pre = first_shape[:axis]\\n685:     first_shape_post = first_shape[axis+1:]\\n686: \\n687:     if any(shape[:axis] != first_shape_pre or\\n688:            shape[axis+1:] != first_shape_post for shape in shapes):\\n689:         raise ValueError(\\n690:             'Mismatched array shapes in block along axis {}.'.format(axis))\\n691: \\n692:     shape = (first_shape_pre + (sum(shape_at_axis),) + first_shape[axis+1:])\\n693: \\n694:     offsets_at_axis = _accumulate(shape_at_axis)\\n695:     slice_prefixes = [(slice(start, end),)\\n696:                       for start, end in zip([0] + offsets_at_axis,\\n697:                                             offsets_at_axis)]\\n698:     return shape, slice_prefixes\\n699: \\n700: \\n701: def _block_info_recursion(arrays, max_depth, result_ndim, depth=0):\\n702:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n703:     Returns the shape of the final array, along with a list\\n704:     of slices and a list of arrays that can be used for assignment inside the\\n705:     new array\\n706: \\n707:     Parameters\\n708:     ----------\\n709:     arrays : nested list of arrays\\n710:         The arrays to check\\n711:     max_depth : list of int\\n712:         The number of nested lists\\n713:     result_ndim : int\\n714:         The number of dimensions in thefinal array.\\n715: \\n716:     Returns\\n717:     -------\\n718:     shape : tuple of int\\n719:         The shape that the final array will take on.\\n720:     slices: list of tuple of slices\\n721:         The slices into the full array required for assignment. These are\\n722:         required to be prepended with ``(Ellipsis, )`` to obtain to correct\\n723:         final index.\\n724:     arrays: list of ndarray\\n725:         The data to assign to each slice of the full array\\n726: \\n727:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n728:     if depth < max_depth:\\n729:         shapes, slices, arrays = zip(\\n730:             *[_block_info_recursion(arr, max_depth, result_ndim, depth+1)\\n731:               for arr in arrays])\\n732: \\n733:         axis = result_ndim - max_depth + depth\\n734:         shape, slice_prefixes = _concatenate_shapes(shapes, axis)\\n735: \\n736:         # Prepend the slice prefix and flatten the slices\\n737:         slices = [slice_prefix + the_slice\\n738:                   for slice_prefix, inner_slices in zip(slice_prefixes, slices)\\n739:                   for the_slice in inner_slices]\\n740: \\n741:         # Flatten the array list\\n742:         arrays = functools.reduce(operator.add, arrays)\\n743: \\n744:         return shape, slices, arrays\\n745:     else:\\n746:         # We've 'bottomed out' - arrays is either a scalar or an array\\n747:         # type(arrays) is not list\\n748:         # Return the slice and the array inside a list to be consistent with\\n749:         # the recursive case.\\n750:         arr = _atleast_nd(arrays, result_ndim)\\n751:         return arr.shape, [()], [arr]\\n752: \\n753: \\n754: def _block(arrays, max_depth, result_ndim, depth=0):\\n755:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n756:     Internal implementation of block based on repeated concatenation.\\n757:     `arrays` is the argument passed to\\n758:     block. `max_depth` is the depth of nested lists within `arrays` and\\n759:     `result_ndim` is the greatest of the dimensions of the arrays in\\n760:     `arrays` and the depth of the lists in `arrays` (see block docstring\\n761:     for details).\\n762:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n763:     if depth < max_depth:\\n764:         arrs = [_block(arr, max_depth, result_ndim, depth+1)\\n765:                 for arr in arrays]\\n766:         return _concatenate(arrs, axis=-(max_depth-depth))\\n767:     else:\\n768:         # We've 'bottomed out' - arrays is either a scalar or an array\\n769:         # type(arrays) is not list\\n770:         return _atleast_nd(arrays, result_ndim)\\n771: \\n772: \\n773: def _block_dispatcher(arrays):\\n774:     # Use type(...) is list to match the behavior of np.block(), which special\\n775:     # cases list specifically rather than allowing for generic iterables or\\n776:     # tuple. Also, we know that list.__array_function__ will never exist.\\n777:     if type(arrays) is list:\\n778:         for subarrays in arrays:\\n779:             yield from _block_dispatcher(subarrays)\\n780:     else:\\n781:         yield arrays\\n782: \\n783: \\n784: @array_function_dispatch(_block_dispatcher)\\n785: def block(arrays):\\n786:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n787:     Assemble an nd-array from nested lists of blocks.\\n788: \\n789:     Blocks in the innermost lists are concatenated (see `concatenate`) along\\n790:     the last dimension (-1), then these are concatenated along the\\n791:     second-last dimension (-2), and so on until the outermost list is reached.\\n792: \\n793:     Blocks can be of any dimension, but will not be broadcasted using\\n794:     the normal rules. Instead, leading axes of size 1 are inserted,\\n795:     to make ``block.ndim`` the same for all blocks.
